title: Data Science Question Set
content:
Data Distribution

Given a multivariate Gaussian distribution defined on a 𝑑-dimensional
space, estimate the probability (up to a constant scaling factor) of
finding a point as a function of distance from the mean. You may assume
the Gaussian distribution is spherical (i.e., its covariance matrix is
given by 𝜎𝐈 ). Plot this for different values of 𝑑 (dimensionality of
space).

In the case of Uniform Distribution, the volume of a sphere
of radius r in D-dimensional space must be a multiple of , which can be written as:  where  depends only on . So we get the ratio: 
After converting the formula into code:
function uniform_curse_dimensionality()    x = 0:0.01:1;    for D = 1: 20        y = 1 - (1-x).^D;        plot(x, y)        legend_str{D} = ['Dimension', num2str(D)];        hold on    end    title("The dimension curse of uniform distribution")    legend(legend_str, Location="best")end
The result is:


Uniform Distribution Curse
Dimensionality

In the case of Gaussian distribution, the integral of the
n-dimensional gaussian distribution is  We first integrate the
univariate Gaussian distribution with radius in : 
Let , we have  Let 
denotes as the thickness of the shell. We can get the final ratio as:

The graph can be generated using the following code:
function gaussian_curse_dimensionality()    x = 0:0.01:1;    for D = 1:20        y = 1-(1-exp(-(1-x).^2)).^(0.5*D);        plot(x, y)        hold on    endend


Spherical Shell Thickness Probability
Distribution


Simulate the distribution of points in MATLAB and empirically
estimate the probability of finding a point as a function of distance.
Compare with previous answer.

function guassian_simulation()    for d = 2:15        mu = zeros(1, d);        Sigma = eye(d);        rng('default')        R = mvnrnd(mu,Sigma,1000);        histogram(sum(R'.*2), 20)        hold on    endend


image

We couldn't get enough information from this graph, so we turned it
into a line graph plot by changing slightly the code as follows:
function guassian_simulation()    for d = 2:6:30        mu = zeros(1, d);        Sigma = eye(d);        rng('default')        R = mvnrnd(mu,Sigma,100000);        [n,x] = hist(sum(R'.*2), 30);        plot(x, n)        legend()        hold on    endend
The result is shown as follows:


Gaussian Simulation Dimension
Plot

We can see that the right half part basically
matches our predicted image. Actually we turn the previous image to the
distance from the center point as follows:


image

The conclusion is: As the dimension increase, the probability that
the point is far from the center gradually increases. However the
increase is not as much as that of the uniform distribution.

In speaker verification systems, it is common to represent speech
utterances as i-vectors which are typically 200-400 dimensional vectors
that nominally follow a standard normal distribution (mean is 𝟎 and
covariance is 𝐈). It has been argued that ‘cosine similarity’ is a good
measure of similarity/dissimilarity between these vectors. Do you agree?
Justify your answer based on your previous plots. Cosine similarity is
defined as below:


I totally agree.
Due to the curse of dimensionality of high-dimensional spaces, for
200-400dimensions, most points are distributed near the spherical shell.
This causes the Euclidean distance difficult to compare the difference
between different points in high dimensional spaces.
Cosine similarity measures the angle of the space vector, and it is
more reflected in the difference in direction, rather than position.
Euclidean distance can reflect the absolute difference of individual
numerical characteristics, so it is more used for analysis that needs to
reflect the difference from the numerical size of the dimension. Thus
cosine similarity is a better than Euclidean similarity in this
case.
Modelling

Implement the EM algorithm to obtain the maximum likelihood estimate
of a Gaussian mixture model given some data (multivariate) in MATLAB
without using any built-in functions. (Hint: The ‘log-sum-exp’ trick may
be useful in avoiding underflow).

We first define the Gaussian Distribution function:
function p = gaussian_function(x, mu, Sigma)    m = size(mu, 2);    p = 1./((2*pi).^(m/2) * sum(sum((Sigma)' * Sigma)).^(1/2)) * exp(-0.5 * (x - mu) * (inv(Sigma)) * (x - mu)');end
Then we figure out the responsibility of the individual Gaussian:
function outp = E_step(X, K, phi, mu, Sigma)    [m, ~] = size(X);    outp = zeros(m, K);    for i = 1:m        for j = 1:K            P(i, j) = phi(j, 1) * gaussian_function(X(i,:), mu(j, :), Sigma(:, :, j));        end        P_sum = sum(P(i, :));        for j = 1:K            outp(i, j) = P(i, j) / P_sum;        end    endend
In the above function, we have to calculate for each , what is the density of probability for
it to appear in  th clustering
group. Here  is a -by- dimensional matrix. Then we should
calculate 
accordingly.
And we define a function(M_step) attempt to maximize the paremeter
phi, u, Sigma:
function [phi, u, Sigma] = M_step(X, respons)    [m, n] = size(X);    K = size(respons, 2);    respons_k = sum(respons, 1);    phi = (respons_k / m)';    for i = 1: K         respons_v = repmat(respons(:,i),1,n);        u(i, :) = sum(respons_v .* X, 1)./ repmat(respons_k(i), 1, n);    end        for i = 1: K        respons_v = repmat(respons(:,i), 1, n);        Sigma(:, :, i) =  (respons_v .* X)' * X ./ repmat(respons_k(i), n, n) - u(i, :)' * u(i, :);    endend

Demonstrate that your implementation works by creating suitable
artificial data (where you know the true distribution) and testing your
implementation on it. You are free to create whatever tests you deem are
suitable to convince someone (like your future boss) that your
implementation works.

We define a DataGenerator function here in order to generate data
according to the input Gaussian parameters, where m is the number of
clustering groups.
function DataGenerator(mu, Sigma, dataNum)    [m, n] = size(mu);     X = zeros(dataNum, n);    for i = 1: dataNum        index = (m-1) * rand(1) + 1;        X(i, :) = mu(index,:) + randn(1, n) * Sigma(:,:,index);    end    save('data.mat', 'X');end
The implementaion of our test is written as follows:
mu = [1 6; 8 2];Sigma(:,:,1) = [2 0; 0 2];Sigma(:,:,2) = [1 0; 0 1];generate_Data(mu, Sigma);iteration = 30;fprintf('Loading and Visualizing Data ...\n')load('EM_GMM_data.mat');[m, n] = size(X);x1 = X(:, 1);x2 = X(:, 2);plot(x1, x2, 'rx', 'MarkerSize', 10, 'LineWidth', 1.5);xlabel('x1');   ylabel('x2');fprintf('Program paused. Press enter to continue.\n');pause;phi_x = 1/K * ones(K, 1);mu_x = rand(K, n);Sigma_x = 2*rand(n,n,K) - rand(n,n,K);for i = 1:K    while (det(Sigma(:,:,i))<1e-2)        Sigma(:,:,i) = 2*rand(n,n) - rand(n,n);    endendphi_last = phi_x;u_last = mu_x;Sigma_last = Sigma_x;for i = 1:iteration    respons = E_step(X, K, phi_last, u_last, Sigma_last);    [phi_new, u_new, Sigma_new] = M_step(X, respons);    phi_last = phi_new;    u_last = u_new;    Sigma_last = Sigma_new;end x1_val = linspace(-5, 15, 100);x2_val = linspace(-5, 15, 100);for i = 1:length(x1_val)    for j = 1:length(x2_val)      x_cb = [x1_val(i), x2_val(j)];          P_d1(i,j) = gaussian_function(x_cb, u_new(1,:), Sigma_new(:,:,1));            endendfor i = 1:length(x1_val)    for j = 1:length(x2_val)      x_cb = [x1_val(i), x2_val(j)];          P_d2(i,j) = gaussian_function(x_cb, u_new(2,:), Sigma_new(:,:,2));            endendP_d = phi_new(1) * P_d1 + phi_new(2) * P_d2;P_d = P_d';figure;surf(x1_val, x2_val, P_d);xlabel('x1'); ylabel('x2');figure;contour(x1_val, x2_val, P_d, logspace(-5, -1, 10));
The result is as follows:


gaussian simulation result

Machine Learning

Build a classifier to distinguish between the sub-species of iris
using data from the Fisher Iris Dataset (available in MATLAB). Use 30%
of the available data as your test set and report your classification
accuracy and confusion matrix. You are free to use any classification
method and feature transformations that you think are suitable to the
problem but explain the reasons for your choice (this can include but is
not restricted to performance comparisons with alternative choices). For
BONUS marks, implement everything yourself without using any built-in
functions or external toolboxes.

First we load and look through the data using
head(Iris):


image

For convenience, we process the labels as data. We can see the number
of categories through the unique() function:


image

We then convert the three categories to 1, 2, 3:
num = length(Iris.Species);label = [];for i = 1:num    switch Iris.Species(i)        case 'Iris-setosa'            label(i) = 1;        case 'Iris-versicolor'            label(i) = 2;        case 'Iris-virginica '            label(i) = 3;    endendlabel = label';data = [Iris.SepalLengthCm,Iris.SepalWidthCm,Iris.PetalLengthCm,Iris.PetalWidthCm];
We made a simple heatmap of the data:
data = rmmissing(data, 1);RHO = corr(data);name =  Iris.Properties.VariableNames(2:5);heatmap(name,name,RHO);colormap hot


image

The heatmap results show that the three variables and Species have
strong correlations. Then we split the data simply:
train_data = [data(1:40,:);data(51:90,:);data(101:140,:)];train_label = [label(1:40,:);label(51:90,:);label(101:140,:)];test_data = [data(1:40,:);data(51:90,:);data(101:140,:)];test_label = [label(1:40,:);label(51:90,:);label(101:140,:)];
Then made the normalization using the mapminmax function:
[mtrain,ntrain] = size(train_data);[mtest,ntest] = size(test_data);dataset = [train_data;test_data];  [dataset_scale,ps] = mapminmax(dataset',0,1);     dataset_scale = dataset_scale';train_data = dataset_scale(1:mtrain,:);test_data = dataset_scale( (mtrain+1):(mtrain+mtest),: );
Finally we made a simple training using the svmtrain
function and the svmpredict function:
model = svmtrain(train_label, train_data,'-c 2 -g 1');[predict_label, accuracy,desc_value] = svmpredict(test_label, test_data, model);
The accuracy is 96.6667%. I think this accuracy rate is quite
satisfactory.
During the coding, I found that there is a code-free toolkit in
matlab that can train and fit a classifier without intervention. The
usage is type classificationLearner in the command
line.
We can generate the scatter graph of several variables directly as
follows:


image

It can be seen that this variable can distinguish the blue points and
other points perfect. After training with Gaussian Naive Bayes, we plot
the validation confusion matrix:


image

The accuracy is 95.3% and the training time is 1.8538 sec. Then we
train with Kernel Naive Bayes, the result is shown as follows:


image

The accuracy is 96% this time while the training time is 1.0172
sec.
Finally we used the Weighted SVM to train the model, the result is
shown as follows:


image

It can be seen that each classifier achieves a quite good
performance. And the results of different classifiers are not much
different.

title: Effective Python
content:
This article is used to improve Python programming skills, based on
some summaries when reading the book Effective Python, and is still
being updated (because the book is still being read slowly). In the
past, I didn’t have many skills in Python programming, and most of my
code followed some habits of C++, which led to the sometimes too verbose
code written. This blog records some common operations or the extraction
of confusing concepts during reading. And also includes some code used
in my own testing.

Bytes and Str
Python has two types that can represent sequences of characters,
bytes and str. A bytes instance contains raw data, an 8-bit unsigned
value. We can directly use ASCII encoding to replace any character we
need.
For example:
a = b'h\x65llo'print(list(a))print(a)
The output is:
[104, 101, 108, 108, 111] b'hello'


ascii

We can easily find that the ASCII value corresponding to e
is 65, which is why the second output is 'hello'. The first line is the
hex form of the corresponding ASCII values. For example, we can
transform 65 into the hex form, which is actually 101. Using the toolkit
in this website is quite convient to achieve this goal:
https://www.rapidtables.com/convert/number/decimal-to-octal.html


hex_digital

While the instances of str contain Unicode code points (also
called code points) that correspond to text characters in human
languages. For example:
a = 'a\u0300 propos'print(list(a))print(a)
The output is:
['a', '̀', ' ', 'p', 'r', 'o', 'p', 'o', 's'] à propos
Unicode encoding can generate a lot of special characters. To convert
Unicode data to binary data, the encode method of str must be called. To
convert binary data to Unicode data, the decode method of bytes must be
called. When calling these methods, you can clearly indicate the
encoding scheme you want to use, or you can use the system default
scheme, usually UTF-8.


str_bytes

Let's take a look at the specific code to understand the conversion
of str and bytes:
a = "T-shirt"b = a.encode("utf-8")c = a.encode("gbk")print(a == b, a == c, b == c)
The output is:
False False True
It seems that different encoding method doesn't make any difference.
However, when we replace a with Chinese or other types of text, we will
see the difference:
a = "T恤"b = a.encode("utf-8")c = a.encode("gbk")print(a == b, a == c, b == c)
The output is:
False False False
When we print a, b, c separately, we will see the following
outputs:
T恤 b'T\xe6\x81\xa4' b'T\xd0\xf4'
That is exactly the difference between different encoding method. The
above conversion between str and bytes is for text content. If there are
other binary content (such as pictures), bytes cannot be decoded into
str. See the exception of the following code:
img = open('str-bytes.jpg', 'rb').read()img.decode('utf8')
It would raise UnicodeDecodeError We can write a helper
function to facilitate our conversion of bytes and str.
def to_str(bytes_or_str):    if isinstance(bytes_or_str, bytes):        result = bytes_or_str.decode("utf-8")    else:        result = bytes_or_str    return resultprint(repr(to_str(b"foo")))print(repr(to_str("stupid")))
The output is:
'foo' 'stupid'
Similarly, we can define a to_bytes function:
def to_bytes(bytes_or_str):    if isinstance(bytes_or_str, str):        result = bytes_or_str.encode("utf-8")    else:        result = bytes_or_str    return result  print(repr(to_bytes(b"foo")))print(repr(to_bytes("stupid")))
The output is:
b'foo' b'stupid'
What we need to pay attention to is that the bytes type and str type
cannot do the same kind of operation. For example, when we perform the
following concatenation operation:
b"foo" + "s"
A TypeError will raise. Determining whether the bytes
and str instances are equal always evaluates to False, even if the two
instances represent exactly the same characters, they are not equal. For
example, in the following example, the strings they represent are
equivalent in ASCII encoding.
print(b"foo" == "foo")
The output will be False. At the same time, instances of
both types can appear on the right side of the % operator to replace the
%s in the format string on the left. However, if the string is of type
bytes, then %s cannot be replaced with an instance of str,
because Python does not know what scheme the str should be encoded in.
But the reverse is possible, that is, if the format string is of type
str, you can replace the %s in it with a bytes instance. The problem is
that this may not be the same as what you want.
print(b"you are %s" % b"foo")print("you are %s" % "intersting")print(b"you are %s" % "foo")print("you are %s" % b"intersting")
The outputs of these statements, in turn, are:
b'you are foo' you are interstingTypeError: %b requires a bytes-like object, or an object that implements __bytes__, not 'str'you are b'intersting'
In addition, if the 'w' mode is specified when calling the open
function, the system will require that it must be written in text mode.
If you want to use binary mode, you should specify 'wb'. In text mode,
the write method accepts str instances containing Unicode data, not
bytes instances containing binary data. So, we have to change the mode
to 'wb' to fix the problem.
Formatted String
Using the % operator to stuff values into a C-style
format string has many problems and is cumbersome to write. Just
like:
templete = "%s loves food. See %s cook"name = "Max"formatted = template % (name, name)print(formatted)
Actually, there are three main troubles using the C-style format
string: 1. If the values in the tuple on the right of %
change in type or order, the program may fail due to incompatibility
when converting types; 2. Before filling in the template, it is often
necessary to do a little processing of the value to be filled in. But in
this way, the whole expression may be written very long, which makes
people feel more confusing; 3. If you want to fill multiple positions in
the format string with the same value, you must repeat the value
correspondingly multiple times in the tuple to the right of the
% operator.
The str.format method uses a set of mini-languages to
define its format specifiers that give us some useful concepts, but in
other respects the method is as diverse as C-style format strings
disadvantages, so we should also avoid using it. The example is as
follows:
template = (    "you are {adj}, "    "your {noun} is beautiful")formatted = template.format(adj="clever", noun="hair")print(formatted)
THe output is:
you are clever, your hair is beautiful
f-string adopts a new way of writing, which fills the
value into the string, which solves the biggest problem caused by the
C-style format string. f-strings is a concise and powerful
mechanism for embedding arbitrary Python expressions directly in format
specifiers. The exmple is as follows:
key = "why you stay here?"value = "because of you"formatted = f"John: {key}\nLucy: {value}"print(formatted)
The output is:
John: why you stay here? Lucy: because of you
Unpacking
Unpacking is a widely used mechanism in Python, and any iterable
object can be split, no matter how many layers of iterative structures
there are in it. The writing method of Unpacking allows us to assign the
elements in the tuple to multiple variables with only one statement:
string1, string2 = ("this is the first string for turple", "this is another string")print(string1)
A great effect of this writing is that we can swap two variables in
place through unpacking without having to create temporary variables. As
an example, for the simplest bubble sort, our traditional implementation
looks like this:
def bubble_sort(a):    for i in range(len(a)):        for j in range(1, len(a)):            if a[j-1] < a[j]:                temp = a[j-1]                a[j-1] = a[j]                a[j] = temp    return ato_sort_list = [2, 4, 6, 3, 12, 6, 2, 3, 432, 1]bubble_sort(to_sort_list)
The result is:
[432, 12, 6, 6, 4, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1]
However, using the unpacking mechanism, we can simplify the code
as:
def bubble_sort(a):    for i in range(len(a)):        for j in range(1, len(a)):            if a[j-1] < a[j]:                a[j], a[j-1] = a[j-1], a[j]    return ato_sort_list = [2, 4, 6, 3, 12, 6, 2, 3, 432, 1]bubble_sort(to_sort_list)
The result is of course the same as before.
Enumerate
It is recommend that we should replace range with enumerate as much
as possible. Let's start with an example. We have a list and we want to
print the position of the element in the list while looping to print
each element. Using the formatted string trick mentioned earlier, we can
write code like this:
food_list = ["banana", "apple", "chicken", "noddle"]for i in range(len(food_list)):    print(f"{i}: {food_list[i]}")
The output is:
0: banana 1: apple 2: chicken 3: noddle
In fact, Python has a built-in function enumerate that can
do this more concisely. Enumerate can wrap any iterator into a lazy
generator:
it = enumerate(food_list)print(next(it))print(next(it))
The output is:
(0, 'banana') (1, 'apple')
Each pair of data output by enumerate can be unpacked into the two
variables of the for statement, which will make the code clearer. The
starting sequence number can be specified by the second parameter of
enumerate (default is 0):
for i, flavor in enumerate(food_list, 1):    print(f"{i}, {flavor}")
The output is:
1, banana 2, apple 3, chicken 4, noddle
Zip
The zip function wraps two or more iterators into a lazy generator.
Each time it loops, it fetches the next element from each of these
iterators and puts those values in a tuple. For example:
names = ["Andy", "Louis", "Sheley", "Chicken"]names_len = [len(i) for i in names]for name, name_len in zip(names, names_len):    print(f"{name}'s length is {name_len}")
The output is:
Andy's length is 4 Louis's length is 5 Sheley's length is 6 Chicken's length is 7
If the lengths of the lists entered into the zip are inconsistent,
the number of loops is actually equal to the length of the shortest
list. Because the design of the zip function determines that as soon as
any one of the iterators is processed, it will stop running.
If we want to traverse the longest string, instead of stopping at the
short one, we can use the zip_longest function built in the itertools
module:
import itertoolsnames = ["Andy", "Louis", "Sheley", "Chicken"]names_len = [len(i) for i in names]names.append("OhNoMatchName")for name, name_len in itertools.zip_longest(names, names_len):    print(f"{name}'s length is {name_len}")
The output is:
Andy's length is 4 Louis's length is 5 Sheley's length is 6 Chicken's length is 7 OhNoMatchName's length is None
for ... else ...
Python's loops have a feature that most programming languages don't
support, namely the ability to place an else block immediately after the
entire loop structure.
The logic of for ... else or while ... else
is that if the code in the for loop jumps out early, the code in the
else will not be run. If the code in the loop runs normally, the code in
the else will also run. Let's consider an example, we can
use this structure when doing prime number determination. First try the
numbers that can divide them at the same time one by one, if you still
can't find such a number after trying all of them, then the loop will be
executed from the beginning to the end (this means that the loop does
not jump out ahead of time because of break), and then the program will
execute the else block.
a = 4b = 9for i in range(2, min(a, b)+1):    print("Testing", i)    if a % i == 0 and b % i == 0:        print("Not coprime")        breakelse:    print("Coprime")

title: Graph Representations Overview
content:
The original intention of writing this article is to record my own
progress in reading the paper when I was conceiving of a transfer
learning graph embedding thesis. The update of this article is based on
real-time paper reading. This article records the key steps and is not
for all readers. More functions are for myself own memo. Therefore, some
of the text is directly taken from the author's paper.

Wasserstein
Weisfeiler-Lehman Graph Kernels
Paper Wasserstein
Weisfeiler-Lehman Graph Kernels(cited 110 times) proposed the graph
Wasserstein distance and how kernels can be derived from it, and also
proposed a seemly out-of-date node embedding method. Thus, above all,
this paper's main contribution is propose a kernel function over graphs
by directly comparing non-parametric node embeddings as point
cloudsthrough optimal transport (Wasserstein distance).
Generate Node Embedding
The specific node embedding method depends on whether the
representation of a node is continuous according to its labels or
features (the authors seem to be a little confused on these two
distinctions). For the labelled non-attributed graph:  While for the continuous attributes
graph:  Let
 and let H be the number of
WL iterations. Then, for every  , the WL features are defined as  where  for
categorically labelled graphs and  for
continuously attributed graphs.  is denoted as the node features
of graph  at iteration  . Then, the node embeddings of graph
 at iteration  are defined as 
Compute Wasserstein distance
The summary of graph Wasserstein distance is shown as follows:


Weisfeiler-Lehman Graph
Embedding

For categorical node features, normalised Hamming distance is used:
 The Hamming distance can be pictured as the
normalised sum of discrete metric  on each of the features. The Hamming
distance equals 1 when two vectors have no features in common and 0 when
the vectors are identical. For continuous node features, Euclidean
distance is employed: 
Compute Wasserstein Graph
Kernel


Algorithm when Computing Wasserstein
Graph Kernel


Def (Wasserstein Weisfeiler-Lehman): Given a set of
graphs  and the GWD defined for each pair of graph on
their WL embeddings, the Wasserstein Weisfeiler Lehman (WWL) kernel is
defined as

title: Urban Simulation
content:
The purpose of writing this article is to record a summary of the key
points of the study of the UCL course Urban Simulation.

Gravitation: The Basic Models
The original gravity model originally suggested by Isaac Newton can
be stated as:

where we define  , and  as
trips, populations, distance squared and a scaling constant.
In fact we can generalise the model first by noting that distance is
like in the von Thunen model a measure of generalised travel cost and
the populations are defined as measures of mass or activity as origin
and destination activities . Then

Where  is the so-called
friction of distance parameter controlling the effect of generalised
travel cost. When  is large,
the effect of distance is great and when it is small it is much less.
This gives more trips when it is small than when it is big.
In all our models, we need to estimate these parameters and this is
the process of calibration. We need to choose  and  so that the predicted trips  are as close as possible to the
observed trips  We can do this in this simplest of models by fitting a
linear regression to the logarithmic version of the model and when we
take logs we get
 In fact we need to make sure the model
generates no more or less than a total of  trips:
 We put them together, and that is:

and from this we can calculate 
as

and the full model is thus

We find the parameters by minimising the sum of the squares (squared
deviations) between the predicted and observed trips, that is

In the 1940, the astronomer John Stewart suggested that a measure of
potential could be produced from the gravity model that was an overall
measure of the force of an object on all others. He defined this from
the basic Gravity Model equation as potential  or potential per capita 
 This is essentially accessibility or nearness and
it was first used as the basis for a simple urban model by Walter Hanson
in the late 1950s in a paper called "How Accessibility Shapes Land Use".
There he said that the residential development in a place was a simple
function of accessibility i.e.

In fact if total residential development is  , then the equation can be written
as

And this is our first operational land use model.
Population Density 1-
Dimensional Model
Now imagine we only have ONE origin and MANY destinations – instead
of flows to everywhere we have flows only from say the city centre, the
CBD.


origin zone city centre

Let us use our negative exponential form of gravity model and then
the model becomes  We can now get rid of the
origin notation which is always the city centre (CBD) and the scaling
constant of proportionality and the origin at zone 1 are always the
same. We thus collapse them into a constant  and the travel cost is thus  . The origin at zone 1 are
always the same. We thus collapse them all into a constant  and the travel cost is thus  . If we call  , the population, then the
model is  Now we might think of the flow as population
density and is we assume the destination variable is the land area, then
this gives us a density, that is 
The Family of Spatial
Interaction Models
We have seen how we can constrain the model to produce specific types
that meet various constraints on the amount of trips attracted to
different locations. Our first and most basic model is unconstrained in
that we ensure that the only constraints the model meets re on the total
trips which are predetermined, already known, that is

Now if we know the trips attracted to the origin, or to the
destination, or to both, then these gives rise to different models.
Including the basic model there are four in all that meet the following.
This is the family.

The Unconstrained Model
 subject to

The Singly-Constrained Models

The Origin- Constrained Model

subject to

The Destination-Constrained Model
 subject to


The Doubly Constrained Model
  and


We can work out the full models by substituting the model on the left
into the constraint on the right

title: Urban Simulation for London Underground
content:
Introduction
This article is to use different urban simulation methods to model
the resilience of London's underground and the spatial interaction. The
first part is using several impact measures, global efficiency and
connected component for example, to model the removal of nodes according
to the rank of several centrality measures. The second part try to
decide the spatial interaction models that is suitable for several
scenarios.
London’s underground
resilience
Topological network
To better investigate into the data, we first plot them into a graph
that the relative distance is kept, which is shown in the following
figure:


london underground

Centrality measures
The three centrality we choose to use to distinguish nodes is degree
centrality, betweenness centrality and closeness centrality. Centrality
is a concept used in social network analysis to measure the degree to
which a point in the network or a person is close to the center in the
entire network. That is to say, by knowing the centrality of a node, the
importance of this node in the network can be judged.
Degree centrality measures the degree to which a
node is connected to all other nodes in a network. For our London
underground network with  nodes,
the degree centrality of node  is
the total number of direct connections between  and other  nodes:

It can be seen from the above formula that the larger the network
size, the higher the maximum possible value of degree centrality. To
remove the effect of network size variation on degree centrality,
normalization is required:

In this standardized degree centrality measurement formula, we use
the degree centrality value of node  divided by the maximum possible number
of connections of other  nodes
to obtain the proportion of network nodes that are directly connected to
node . The larger the ratio, the
higher the centrality. For our London underground network, if a station
has a higher degree centrality, it will have more links to other
stations.
Betweenness centrality is used to measure the number
of times a vertex appears in the shortest path between any other pair of
vertices, thereby characterizing node importance. When it comes to
London underground, if a station is between multiple stations, it can be
considered that the node plays an important "intermediary" role, and the
station in this position can control the nearby stations and influence
the group.
It is calculated as follows:

Where  indicates that
the shortest path from  to  passes through , that is,  is on the shortest path from  to .
Likewise, normalization can be done on :

The denominator represents the number of paths between two points in
the graph, that is, the number of all paths.
Closeness centrality reflects how close a station is
to other stations in the network. That is, for a underground station,
the closer it is to other nodes, the higher its closeness
centrality.
It is calculated as follows:

where  represents the
distance from node  to node .
Obviously, this definition is more geometrically in line with the
concept of centrality than degree centrality, because the average
shortest distance to other nodes is the smallest, which means that this
node is geometrically located in the center of the graph.
These three centrality measures are calculated separately in the
London Underground network data. The result of head(10) is shown in the
table below:



node


degree centrality


betweenness centrality


closeness centrality

After choosing the node that own the greatest centrality of the three
centrality measures, we generate table below:



degree centrality

closeness centrality

Euston

Baker Street


Angel




Canning Town


Finchley Road


Moorgate



The intersection of the three measurement, which is King's Cross St.
Pancras, Bank and Monument, Green Park and Waterloo, is also computed.
These four subway stations are among the top in the three centrality
measures, indicating that their great importance.
Impact measures
The two methods to measure impact of removal we choose here is global
efficiency and connected component.
Global efficiency is the average of the inverse
shortest path lengths of all node pairs. Such metrics can be viewed as
measures of the efficiency with which information is exchanged in the
network. Assuming that  is
the shortest path of node  and
node , then the definition is as
follows:

When every station in the London underground network is reachable to
each other, the global efficiency is the largest. And when the shortest
path becomes longer, the global efficiency will gradually decrease. Thus
removing nodes my cause the global efficiency to decrease.
Number of connected subgraphs has the same meaning
of largest connected component. Since it's much easier for networkx
package to implement it, this measurement is adopted instead of
computing the common-see largest connected component. Removing some key
nodes in London underground network may cause the whole graph to be
split into two or more parts, which will increase the number of
connected subgraphs.
Node removal
For the centrality measures we choose, two removal strategies are
adopted to remove the nodes one by one. Strategy A will remove the nodes
according to the nodes ranked, namely non-sequential removal. While
strategy B remove one node a time and recompute the most important one
after the removal, and remove it accordingly next time. Strategy B is
so-called sequential removal.
The comparison graph of Strategy A is shown in figure below:


top_compare_GC_unweighted

It can be seen that removing nodes according to the three centrality
measures will lead to a rapid decline in global efficiency, but removing
nodes according to closeness centrality does not increase the connected
component, that is, the graph obtained after removing the nodes is still
a connected graph, and the stations are still connected to each other.
reachable. In contrast, removing nodes according to the other two
centralities will immediately make the graph disconnected, indicating
that the importance of removing nodes by these two methods has a greater
impact on the connectivity of the whole graph than closeness centrality.
It can also be seen in the graph of global efficiency that the removal
of nodes selected according to closeness centrality has a smaller impact
on the whole graph than the other two methods. Thus the degree
centrality and betweenness centrality are both good choice for
reflecting the importance of a station for the functioning of the
underground.
The comparison of Strategy B is shown in figure below:


top_compare_GC_unweighted_sequential

Compared to the previous figure, we can find that strategies B has
significantly higher declines in global efficiency and higher connected
components than strategy A. Actually this is quite understandable.
Because strategy B actually finds the most important node under the
current centrality measure for deletion every time a node is deleted,
strategy A relies on the order of the nodes selected at the beginning to
delete, thus ignoring different nodes in the deletion process change in
importance. Therefore, I think Strategy B can more truly reflect the
importance ranking of deleting nodes, thus is more effective at studying
resilience.
As far as I am concern, directly analyzing the number of connected
subgraphs is a better way to assess the damage. Because its meaning is
more intuitive and obvious, it represents whether removing a node will
affect the connectivity of the entire graph and the magnitude of the
effect (how many subgraphs are formed). In contrast, in the global
efficiency, although we can see that the value has been decreasing, it
is difficult for us to directly see which node deletion has a greater
impact.
Flows: weighted network
The degree centrality and measurement discussed above ignored the
influence of the flows between stations. We only take the connection
activity into account.
Centrality measures
Since we need to use the distance between nodes as a metric in
closeness centrality, the flow between stations is provided in the
original data. We convert the 0 part of the flows to a relatively small
value (such as 1), and then take the inverse of the flows to get the
distance.


0 value

Since the semantics of degree centrality plus weights are ambiguous,
we only calculate betweenness centrality and closeness centrality in
this step of experiments. The comparison between weighted centrality and
original centrality is shown in table below:



betweenness centrality


weighted betweenness centrality


closeness centrality


weighted closeness centrality

We can see that the rank results before and after weighting are
different. But the top 10 stations remain approximately the same.
Impact measures
The global centrality defined above originally supports the weighted
data, we just need to replace the shortest distance with the weighted
one, while the largest connected component remains the same.
Node removal
We implement the experiment as the former one, and we add the former
impact measurement result of closeness centrality and betweenness
centrality into comparison. The result is shown in figures below:


top_compare_GC_weighted



top_compare_GC_weighted_sequential

We found that although the results of weighted and unweighted methods
would indeed be different, there is no clear evidence that one method is
better.
But we found that for both weighted betweenness centrality and
weighted closeness centrality, removing the first few nodes cannot
change the connectivity of the graph. For between centrality, the order
of Stratford stations before and after adding weights is replaced, and
we guess that Stratford station may be a key node. We found the location
of Stratford in the graph and found that it is the center of several
nearby nodes, and at the same time it has the highest degree centrality
and betweenness centrality. Thus we now believe Stratford is an
important node. Remove Stratford will have the largest affect to
passengers.


Stratford

Spatial Interaction models
In this part we will mainly use four models derived from the gravity
model to calibrate the parameter beta based on the actual scenario. Our
data is shown as table below:



station origin

Models and calibration
Briefly
Introduce the Spatial Interaction Models
The family of spatial interaction models (Fotheringham, 1989) are
derived from the gravity model, which can be written as:

subject to

This is also known as the unconstrained model. The singly-constrained
models have two branch, one is the origin-constrained model:

subject to

the other one is destination-constrained model:

subject to

The final one is the doubly constrained model, which is formulated
as:

subject to

 denotes the flows from the
original station , while  denotes the flows to the destination
station . ,  and  are balancing factors such that  can satisfy the constraints.,  and  are three important parameters
that need to be estimated, which will be calculated in the next
part.
The unconstrained model needs to adjust both  and  (and  of course, we will not mention it
again later) to calibrate the model, because it does not retain any
information about original and destination stations. In a single
constraint model, only  or
 will need to be adjust.
Since the double-constrained model contains both the original and
destination stations' information, we will only need to adjust the .
Select
a Spatial Interaction Model and Calibrate the Parameter Beta
We choose to use the unconstrained model according to our scenario.
We first calculate the log function of the formula and rewrite it
into:

Therefore, we first convert the data items population, jobs and
distance of the london_flows.csv data representing ,  and  into log form respectively.
We adopted a generalized linear model regression to fit the function,
the summary is shown in figure below:


summary

From the summary we can see that the  calculated is 0.4197, the  is 0.3277, the  is -0.3005. We also calculated the
RMSE and R-square of the model, which is 108.334 and 0.3212
respectively.
Besides, we can also estimate the coefficient of the production
constrained model. The  is
0.7686 and the  is -0.8781.
The RMSE is 102.893 and R-square is 0.3883.

title: Optimal Transportation Theory
content:
This article is mainly based on the notes taken by Mr. Gu Xianfeng
during his online class on transmission theory. There may be some
clerical errors in it. This is caused by the pursuit of the efficiency
of recording. At the same time, this article also contains some
sentences excerpted from reading other related papers, which are not
listed one by one for convenience. Finally, I sincerely thank Mr. Gu for
his lectures.

Duality Theory
The duality theory was built first by Monge. After hundreds of years,
Kantorovich made an important breakthrough, which earned him the Nobel
Prize in Economics. His theory was later developed into the known
Duality Theory. Brenier, who went a step further and reduced the problem
to the Monge-Ampere equation, finally developed this theory into the
Brenier theory.
In a nutshell, Monge poses the optimal transport map problem. But the
solution of this problem does not necessarily exist, so Kantorovich
reduced the problem condition so that the solution must exist and made
it possible to be processed by linear program. The duality theory used
the General Lagrange Multiplier to add the constraint into the energy
and transformed it into a duality problem, which can be transformed into
Euler Equation(PDE) by c-transform. Brenier finally transformed the PDE
into Monge-Ampere equation.
There is a question left whether these questions are equal. Thus the
first thing we should do is to prove the equivalence of the Kantorovich
problem and the dual problem, which will be proved by
cycle-monotonous theory. Brenier problem is actually a
special case, we need to prove that the generalization of Brenier
problem and Kantorovich problem are equivalent to each other in a
sense., which will be proved by polar decomposition
theory. The equivalence of the Kantorovich problem and the
Monge problem will be proved by twisting condition.
After proving the equivalence, we will further prove the stability of
the solution and its generalization(Is the solution unique when relaxing
the condition).
Monge Problem
We first make some assumptions about space. We assume X, Y are two
complete (any cauchy sequence has a limit point) and
separable or detachable(countable
dense subsets exist). Given 2 probability measures, , where  denotes the infinate space composed
of all probability measures in the  space, and cost function  It
should be noted that the objective space is . The MP problem is: >
Given a map  from , which may be smooth,
continuous, or measurable. Is there exist another map  such that  ? The answer to this
question is yes, and there are infinitely many solutions to this
problem. So we want to know if there is a best map.
Let's do some further explanation. Given a(any) measurable subset
, the total mass of the
preimage is equal to the total mass of the image. That is:  Then we say
. How to define the
best mapping? What we want is for each monocule  map to  there is a distance given by the
cost function , and we
want the total cost is the smallest, so we introduce a new concept:
Transport Cost.  Monge's problem is how to
find the map that has the smallest  among all guaranteed measures maps,
which is:  We want to know, whether
the solution exists. If so, whether the solution is unique. If it is
unique, how to find the solution? This question remained unresolved for
hundreds of years. The central difficulty is:  is not closed, which means even though the function
 decreases monotonically,
 may not exist in the space. So we have to ask, what is a
convergent mapping? We will thus introduce weak convergence. Let us
explain it from an economic point of view. We assume that  is the producer and  is the consumer. We want to deliver
the produced goods to consumers, but the distribution of  and  is not uniform, some places produce
more, and some places consume less. How do we design a transmission
scheme that balances supply and demand? Of course, there are infinitely
many such transmission schemes. How do we design the transmission method
to minimize the freight, which is the optimal transmission problem. We
will define the Wasserstein Distance, which gives the total cost of
optimal transport, as:   This is the classic formulation of the Monge-Ampere
problem. However, this formulation has a fatal flaw. In reality, a
producer can provide products to multiple consumers, causing this to no
longer be a mapping. Thus, we need to relax the map into a scheme, or we
can say plan. The essential difference between map and scheme is whether
a point can be splited, which is also the main idea of Kantorvich's
theory. Now we have to think about how to express a transport scheme.
Generally speaking, a transmission scheme can be expressed as a joint
probability distribution . Recalling that the sum of the goods
produced by the producer to all consumers, we get:   Similarly,  which also means . This is the
marginal distribution of .

We examine all joint probability distributions that satisfy We call
such a probability distribution a transmission scheme

We can clearly see that the transfer map is a special case of the
transfer scheme, and the transfer scheme is a generalization of the
transfer map. Thus we can see the transfer map as a special joint
probability distribution , such that satisfied . Generally the
support is high-dimensional. Map is a causality while scheme is
a correlation. Thus in machine learning, what many people are
using is optimal transport. But if we learn the Brenier theory, we will
find that the result we get is actually a transport map. It's important
to distinguish causality from correlation since the latter one will be
more computationally expensive.
Kantorvich Theory
Actually,  is a
convex space. That is, . Thus the space is also a simply connected space.
> (KP) Given a transportation scheme, the cost is as follows: >

We can see that this question is a linear one, for that:  and for the constraint, we get:  is also a
linear one. So this problem is transformed into a minima problem of
finding a linear function on a convex set. We can find its extremum by
translating the linear function, but it is not necessarily unique. And
the theory can be naturally applied to both discrete and continuous
cases, unlike traditional differential geometry. That is, for continuous
cases, , it might
be like:  For discrete cases, it might be like:
 Now we need to prove the existence of
the solution to Kantorvich Problem. The core idea of the proof is
simple. We have a convex set , and linear functionals must find the maxima and minima on
the convex set. But this convex set is an infinite-dimensional space in
which we need to find a sequence of points that converges to a minima.
The problem then boils down to how do we prove that this space is
compact, how to define distances and Cauchy columns in the space, and
how to prove that the Cauchy column converges and the limit of
convergence is still in the space.

Def (Seperable Space): Topological Space X, if there
exists a dense subset , such that  open set, .

For example, a rational number on the real axis is a dense set.
Because any open set on the real axis must have a rational number.

Def (Compact): 

That is to say, Compact means that the space is closed for seeking
the limit. The space of rational numbers is not compact, we can find
rational numbers that approximate , but  is not a
rational number.

Def (Norm): 


Def (Cauchy Sequence): 

In fact, the core skill of functional analysis is how to choose the
norm. Different norm of the same set will lead to different
convergence.

Def (Complete):  Cauchy Sequence , , and .

For example, the open set 
is noncomplete, while the closed set  is complete. The difficulty of the Monge problem is that the
admissible solution space is noncomplete. Taken together, our
requirements for space are linear, normed, complete,
which is the Banach Space. There are two kinds of convergence in Banach
space, strong convergence and weak convergence. Strong convergence means
that the Cauchy column converges in Banach space. Sometimes the meaning
of this convergence is too strong, so we introduce weak convergence.

Def (Dual Space):  is a vector space, dual space 

It can be shown that the dual of a dual space is equal to itself when
the space dimension is finite. Dual spaces are introduced to define
generalized functions. Sometimes it is difficult to study the
convergence of the original space, but it converges in the dual space.
We denote 

Def (Weak Convergence): Given a Banach Space and a
sequence  weakly converge to
, denoted as , iff , we have .

That is to say, we examine the weak convergence of a sequence, and
examine whether the effects of their actions on the dual space are the
same. Similarly, we can also study the convergence of a functional
sequence.

Def (Weak - Convergence): Given a Banach Space and a sequence
 weakly converge to , denoted as , iff , we have .


Theorem (Banach - Alaoglu):  is a Separable Banach Space, , 

In other words, dual space of separable Banach spaces is
compact.

Def (lower - semi - continous): Given a metric space
, ,  convergent sequence , we have 

SImilarly we can define upper-semi-continous:

Def (upper - semi - continous):  continuous   lower simi continuous and  upper semi continuous


Def ():
 is a metrix space,  is continuous, euqals to 0 as
,

 is a confusing concept.
In another words,  if
and only if  and ,  compact , , . We can
define a norm on , that is:
  under this norm is a Banach space.
We can also define , that is:
  under  is compact metric
space.

Def (Signed measure): Given a metric space ,  is a signed finite measure.  Borel set ,  assigns a real number ,  countable disjoint union , , we
haveand   :=


We can generate the positive signed measure as:

title: 和茜茜去云南
content:
假期去云南，这个计划是和茜茜在120（宿舍楼）楼下的老地方“架膝长谈”时候花了三分钟定的。茜茜当时还在准备着考试，而我毕业季也忙的很，计划一直拖到出发前两天才匆匆定下，这也导致了前两天我们踩了一些坑。但和茜茜出去旅游最棒的一点就是，俩人都很有默契，即使是旅途中有多多少少的差强人意，我们也能迅速调整好重新出发。我们最后的行程与最初的计划截然不同，但回想起这七天，实在是一场很棒的旅行~

行程安排
第一天：广州飞机到昆明（昆明没什么玩的，可以直接中转去其他地方），若实在有空可以下飞机后中转到海洪湿地公园划小船，提前查询时间段内是否有海鸥，若有就到滇池喂海鸥（不要坐索道上去，滇池就在下面，千万不要去西山公园和茶马花街，特别无聊），若在昆明呆一个晚上，可以住在南强路附近，那里有小吃街，生活气息比较浓，而且第二天坐高铁去丽江或大理方便。
第二天：下午高铁前往大理，可以感受晚上11点之后古城内路边青年民谣喝酒摆摊的氛围，这是丽江和独克宗都没有的。当天晚上租电动车或敞篷车准备第二天环洱海，具体的路线直接问租车的老板，会介绍的很详细。若租电动车一定要租锂电池的（200公里及以上的），价格大约100元，建议直接高德地图找康辉旅游租电动车。古城里的酒吧基本点一杯酒都是100左右，可以去大冰的小屋体验一下，较实惠而且氛围很好。大理古城里外卖的吃的都不咋好吃，没啥必要买。记得旺季吃午饭提前预约一下，看看美团排名前几的几家菌锅都不错，服务也很好，比如清宏私厨、洱月小厨、源汁源味等，都挺不错的。住宿可以住在大理梵居栖溪汀苑民宿，这家店是在古城外面，但环境很好，晚上可以在院子拍拍照，可以住那个大一点的房型，价格贵的不算太多，体验很好。
第三天：（骑电动环洱海版，租敞篷车问问老板）早上可以睡晚一点，大约11点吃完饭开始环洱海，直接从大理往喜洲古城去，大约20公里，中间会经过磻溪村（网红S弯），可以骑进去逛逛拍几张照片，但不用呆太久，因为环海西路主要就是骑单车玩的，租电动或者租车没必要呆太久，没必要租自行车拍照，看起来太蠢。可以买帽子，记得砍价（我们买的是30元2顶，价格还可以啦）。喜洲古城可以逛逛，那里的东西稍微便宜一些，去大麦田那拍拍照，路边的咖啡店也可以拍拍照，咖啡不知道多少钱，但觉得没有必要。附近有些扎染体验店，可以看看别人怎么搞的，但自己尝试就没多大必要，我们试了一个85块钱扎染袋子的，流程就是选一下想染的图案，用线扎起来，然后他会浸到板蓝根水里染色，然后就完事了，体验一般。随后往环海东路的双廊古城骑，那边也可以逛逛，但也都差不多，双廊古城如果车骑不进去就抬进去，问题不大没人查的，可以骑出来。环海东路可以好好拍照，一路风景都很美，中间可以下车去近海的位置拍拍照，有些地方甚至可以下到海边，有树有海有阳光风景很棒。其实环洱海最美的就是路上，而不是某个景区，所以不要对那些目的地节点抱太高的期望，享受路上就好。下一站可以在小普陀停一下看看，其实就是一条延到海里的小路，还可以但也就那样。然后去理想邦，理想邦如果能在下午快落日的时候去就最好了，那里很出片，多拍拍照。然后就往洱海公园那边走，这个时候应该是黄昏，路边多停下拍拍照，这个时候应该是最舒服的时候了。然后就一直往酒店的方向导航就成。晚上逛逛古城吃点东西，早点回去休息，应该会比较累。
第四天：前往丽江，丽江古城若有行李建议住在外面，古城里的石板路拖行李比较麻烦，而且不方便打车等交通方式，若住古城里面记得提前问老板能不能接。建议到了丽江古城体验一下盲人按摩，手法非常不错。丽江古城卖的东西相比起其他便宜很多，可以尝尝鲜花饼和云南小粒咖啡和果酒等。去酒吧记得别太晚，太晚很多都关门或者不唱歌了，就没意思了。丽江吃的东西都很便宜，直接美团搜一下就好，我们呆的时间不是很久所以没有太多建议。
第五天：丽江前往香格里拉（直接坐客运车过去），香格里拉就住松赞林寺附近可以，也可以住古城里面。下午可以去松赞林寺走走，然后晚点就坐大巴环一下纳帕海（2元1位），没必要租车环或骑马，有些时候水位会很高，很有意思，但租电动车环就很惨。晚上就逛逛独克宗古城，那里的环境和其他古城还是有区别的，可以编个彩辫也可以找个旅拍拍一下照，下午七点四方街那大家都在一起跳舞，可以一起去跳一下，大转轮那晚上可以拍拍照，还蛮好看的，但藏族人认为这是盗版的，不要太当真就好。第二天如果想去巴拉格宗可以报个团，比较便宜，当日来回。我们第二天去的是德钦雨崩村，所以没去巴拉格宗。
第六天：如果不去德钦雨崩村的话，可以去附近的玉龙雪山（提前查还有没有雪），因为这次7月份去，玉龙雪山没雪了，我们就去德钦的梅里雪山，住的话如果不准备去雨崩村徒步可以住在飞来寺，那边交通挺方便，直接从香格里拉坐客运车就能到，第二天运气好可以看到日照金山，提前定好闹钟，定好可以直接看到雪山的酒店。
第七天：如果前一天住在雨崩村，可以在雨崩村徒步。进出雨崩村直接坐越野车，不要徒步，200到上雨崩，260到下雨崩，回程上雨崩150，雨崩村的住宿和吃饭价格都相对较高而且质量一般，要有预期。徒步有三天线路，神瀑、神湖、冰湖，难度递减，期望不要太高，神瀑的路线相对更成熟。记得在酒店免费借登山杖，不用自己带。具体路线的攻略自己查就行，网上很多。需要注意的是记得穿多一点，带多点水，走快一点，多问路。住宿如果图方便最好住下雨崩，上雨崩交通便宜一点，但相对要走的多一些（去徒步需要经过下雨崩，从上雨崩到下雨崩得走40分钟左右，路比较难走而且比较累，越野车到下雨崩价格比上雨崩贵60块），如果在雨崩村多住几天可以尝试两三条线路，下午可以在下雨崩喝个咖啡，可以骑个马什么的，但价格要有预期。
注意事项

环洱海和很多地方都记得涂防晒，套冰袖
云南早晚温差大，外出记得带外套，厚薄看个人，如果骑电动环洱海更得带，不然冷死
很多路程要么拼车要么坐客运大巴，拼车提前联系好司机，直接找客栈老板要司机电话，然后找司机问有没有拼车的，或者让他给介绍。如果从德钦回香格里拉价格80-100一人，出了雨崩村直接定就好。实际上可以电话里联系好一辆车，现场看到有车就直接上（虽然有点不道德，但可以避免电话里联系的车需要等别人太久，司机很会说大话的，也可以避免现场没车），从德钦客运站到雨崩村坐越野车点可以砍价到大约120一辆车，很难拼到车，时间充裕也可以坐客运大巴过去，自己看时间怎么衔接就好
环洱海不要报团，自己去就成
住洱海边要小心蚊子，提前问问老板和当地人，要住的话可以住在龙龛码头，看日出什么的好看，看日落可以去才村码头，如果有时间的话
去雨崩村的越野车两个人的话坐中间最好，不要坐最后一排，坐到吐而且没什么好看的，第一排只能坐一个人。另外等车时间比较长，如果赶车需要提前预留好时间提前到！

彩蛋

雨崩村徒步路线下山点有一家客栈，当时我们吃的松茸特别便宜（可能是运气好而已，不一定可复制），那边是直接采松茸的地方，可以在那买点松茸
当地人都特别好，有不懂的多问人，想了解路线更详细的信息找当地的旅行社多问问，报团就慎重，因为很多没什么必要抱团，除非太远的地方
泸沽湖比较远，但风土人情有意思而且天气好的话很美，可以在那住一个晚上
西双版纳没去，下次准备去
一些其他可选景点：昆明石林、香格里拉虎跳峡等等
香格里拉风景最好，路上少睡觉多看看窗外拍照

咔咔咔

在云南的路上随处可以见到大大小小的牛躺在路中间，车不得绕道而行，牛就甩着尾巴斜眼瞥着人，好不牛气！

我们到达香格里拉第一站就去了纳帕海，我们叫了个滴滴环湖一周，实际上有环湖巴士的，我们也是之后才知道。记得去纳帕海千万不要骑电动车，因为水位可能比你想象中的要高很多很多，我们开着车过去司机都说喇叭进水了哑了
 在纳帕海边茜茜被海风捧起的头发

我们住在雨崩村猫在客栈，价格挺贵，但确实窗外风景很美，老板找不到钥匙还给我们升级了超级房型！但雨崩村那吃的真的又不好又贵~~~

我们去神瀑路上人们一直和我们说不要再走啦，不要再走啦，再走天黑啦回不来啦，但我们只在那呆一个下午，拖着也得走完呀！茜茜特别棒，一路都没有喊累没有喊疼，回到酒店脚都起水泡了，疼了好几天！

我们四点出发，经历了多次走岔路，到了七点二十终于到达了神瀑，虽然风景没有预想中的美，但沿路的经历实在难忘，最后我们喝了一口神瀑的水，希望未来一年能健健康康万事顺遂！
 捕捉一个在艰难走路的茜茜

在香格里拉第一站的独克宗古城，下午七点的四方街人们翩翩起舞


彩虹农庄摆拍


在大理的第三天，我们应茜茜的强烈要求去了彩虹农庄，这里真的很出片，老板人也挺热情，一直给我们拍照，这几张拍的真的像蜜月照，带上茜茜拍照以后都压根不用找旅拍，太美了~
